mnemonics.title: Replication Types
mnemonics.content: SAR - Synchronous (Strong), Asynchronous (Avail), Replication for availability and consistency

mnemonics.title: Master-Slave Benefits
mnemonics.content: HAM: High Availability, Acknowledge writes, Master handles writes, Slave reads scale

acronyms.usage: Transaction log that records changes before applying to database, essential for replication in PostgreSQL
Theorem stating only two of three can be guaranteed in distributed systems
System design ensuring services remain accessible despite failures
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time, determined by replication lag
Maximum acceptable time to restore service after failure
Technique allowing concurrent reads while maintaining consistency in replicated databases

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: Sync = Master waits for ALL replicas (ladder diagram showing parallel confirmation). Async = Master proceeds immediately, replicas update later (staggered timeline)
Star = sun with planets. Chain = linked circles. Ring = circle with arrows both directions. Mesh = complete graph with all connections
Show master timeline ahead of replica timeline, with gap representing lag
Show three states: Normal (Master→Replicas), Failure (Master down, gap), Recovery (Replica promoted)

analogies.analogy: Like a group photo where everyone confirms they are ready before the photo is taken - everyone has the same moment captured.
Like sending emails - you send (commit locally), then it propagates to others at different times.
Like a head office (master) making all decisions, with branch offices (slaves) having read-only access to head office data.
Two people editing the same Google Doc offline - when they sync, conflicts must be resolved.
Like weather report delay - the weather (data) has changed, but your display (replica) hasn't updated yet.
